content.problemStatement:
Ada is one of the best hairdressers in ChefLand. She invented a very cool hair style and named it "power tree".

A power tree with degree lll — let's denote it by PT(l)PT(l)PT(l) — is a directed graph, which can be constructed recursively as follows:


PT(0)PT(0)PT(0) consists of a single node.
For l>0l \gt 0l>0:

Create one node uuu — the root of power tree PT(l)PT(l)PT(l).
Construct all power trees with smaller degrees, i.e. PT(0),PT(1),…,PT(l−1)PT(0), PT(1), \dots, PT(l-1)PT(0),PT(1),…,PT(l−1).
For each of the tree created in the previous step, add a directed edge from uuu to its root.


Today, Ada is going to cut the hair of some customers. Of course, all of them want to have a power tree hair style!

A customer's uncut hair is represented by a directed graph with NNN vertices (numbered 111 through NNN) and exactly one edge between each pair of vertices. Ada should remove some edges (possibly none) from this graph in such a way that the resulting graph is a power tree. Note that she is not allowed to remove vertices!

Can you help Ada find the edges she should remove?
constraints: There is exactly one edge between each pair of vertices
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains a single integer N. Each of the next N choose 2 lines contains two space-separated integers u and v describing a directed edge from vertex u to vertex v.
content.outputFormat: For each test case: If it is impossible to create a power tree, print a single line containing one integer -1. Otherwise, print two lines. The first of these lines should contain a single integer e denoting the number of edges to remove. The second line should contain e space-separated integers denoting the numbers of the edges to remove.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Ada should remove edges (1,4), (2,4) and (3,1). The resulting graph is a power tree with degree l=2.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a recursive approach to construct the power tree. We start by creating a single node for the power tree with degree 0. Then, for each degree from 1 to l, we create a new node and add edges from this node to the roots of all power trees with smaller degrees. We can use a depth-first search (DFS) to traverse the graph and check if it is possible to create a power tree by removing some edges.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^2) - where T is the number of test cases and N is the number of vertices in the graph. The time complexity is dominated by the DFS traversal of the graph.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) - where N is the number of vertices in the graph. The space complexity is dominated by the recursive call stack of the DFS traversal.
